topic: Nour video call from London — rain and homesickness

turns.content: just got off a video call with my best friend and I'm feeling all the feelings. she's been in London and tbh I think she's struggling more than she lets on
It sounds like the call left you with some mixed emotions. What's going on with her — is it the adjustment to a new city, or something more specific?

I think it's everything?? like she called me and first thing I notice is its POURING rain behind her through the window and she's wearing like 3 layers and a blanket and I'm sitting here in a tank top with the AC on. the visual contrast alone was sending me. but then she started talking about how she hasn't found her people yet and how the office culture is so different and I could tell she was trying not to cry

That's really hard to watch, especially when it's someone you're close to. The "trying not to cry" thing is always harder to see than actual crying — it means she's been holding it together and you're the person she feels safe enough to almost let go with.

The social isolation in a new city is brutal. It's one thing to move for an exciting opportunity, but the day-to-day reality of not having your people around hits different once the novelty wears off. How long has she been there?

a few months now. and like I KNOW she's talented and she's going to be fine eventually but right now she's in that awful in-between phase where everything feels temporary and lonely. she said the other day she went to a cafe alone on Saturday and just sat there for 4 hours because she didn't have anywhere else to be and that broke my heart wallah

The solo cafe for four hours — yeah, that's the loneliest kind of Saturday. It's not even that going to a cafe alone is bad, it's the *not having anywhere else to be* part. The absence of options rather than the choice of solitude.

You're right that she'll be fine eventually. A few months is still very early for building a genuine social circle in a new city, especially one like London where people tend to already have established friend groups. But knowing she'll be fine *eventually* doesn't help the ache of watching her go through it *now*.

What did you say to her? Sometimes it's hard to know the right thing to say when someone's struggling from far away.

I told her to try joining a gym class or something where she'd see the same people regularly because that's how you build friendships as an adult right?? and I told her I'd come visit soon which I genuinely want to do. but I also felt kind of guilty because like. I'm here in Abu Dhabi where I have my whole life and my family and my routine and she's out there being brave and lonely. I mean who's really doing the harder thing you know

The gym class suggestion is actually solid — repeated proximity is one of the strongest predictors of friendship formation. Same people, same time, shared physical activity. It's the adult equivalent of how friendships form naturally in school.

But I want to push back gently on the guilt. You're framing it as she's being "brave" and you're just... comfortable? That's not fair to either of you. Staying and building where you are takes its own kind of courage. And being the friend who's a stable anchor back home — the one she can call and almost cry to — that's not a passive role. You're part of what's keeping her grounded.

Also, the grass-is-always-greener thing works in both directions. She might look at your life and think "Zara has it all figured out" while you look at hers and think she's the brave one. Neither version is the full picture.

ok that actually made me tear up a little ngl. you're right I do sometimes feel like staying here is the less impressive choice but it's more complicated than that. and she did say on the call that she misses our Friday dinners and that when she's having a bad day she thinks about us sitting in her car eating shawarma in the Corniche parking lot at like midnight lol

Midnight shawarma in a parked car on the Corniche — that's the kind of friendship ritual that no amount of London prestige can replace. Those specific, unglamorous little traditions are what people actually miss when they move. Not the skyline or the restaurants, but the car and the shawarma and the person next to them.

The fact that she reaches for that specific memory when she's having a bad day tells you everything about what your friendship means to her. That's not something she's going to find easily in London, and it's not something you should feel guilty about being the keeper of.

When you visit her, you should absolutely bring shawarma supplies and recreate it on a bench somewhere in the rain. It would be terrible and perfect.

LMAO recreating Corniche shawarma in the London rain would be so unhinged and she would absolutely love it. I'm actually gonna plan this visit properly, maybe during Eid break. she needs something to look forward to and honestly so do I. I miss her so much it's annoying like just come back already!!

An Eid visit is a great idea — it gives both of you a countdown, and having something concrete on the calendar makes the distance feel more manageable. Plus you'd get to see her actual life there, meet the coworkers she complains about, judge her neighborhood, the whole thing.

And the "I miss her so much it's annoying" energy is the most honest thing. You don't have to be graceful about it. You can be annoyed that your best friend is in a different time zone and also be proud of her and also wish she'd just come home. All of those things fit in the same feeling.

Start looking at flights before the Eid prices spike — you know how that goes.

true the prices get insane if you wait. yalla I'm gonna text her rn and tell her I'm coming for Eid no excuses. thanks for letting me process all that, I came in feeling heavy and now I feel like I have a plan at least

That's the best outcome — heavy feelings turned into a plan. Go text her, she's probably still up overthinking the call too. And when you visit, take a photo of the rainy shawarma recreation. That's going to be a core memory.

Hope you both feel a little lighter tonight.
